case_name: MTBE removal from contaminated groundwater for municipal drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for MTBE-contaminated groundwater intended for drinking water supply.

input_data.scenario_background: A municipal wellfield has detected methyl tert-butyl ether contamination associated with historical gasoline releases. The utility needs a reliable treatment approach to produce drinking water with acceptable taste and odor and compliance-focused finished water quality. Operators prefer established, full-scale technologies that are commonly used for volatile organic compounds in groundwater treatment.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve strong MTBE reduction suitable for drinking water production, including control of taste and odor impacts, using a dependable treatment train.
secondary_goals: avoid unnecessarily high energy demand where practical
provide stable performance under continuous municipal operation

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: transfer volatile MTBE from water to air using packed tower or equivalent aeration contact
core_unit_functions.offgas_treatment_as_needed: control stripped VOC emissions where required by air regulations or site conditions
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological protection for drinking water distribution

applicability_rationale: MTBE is a volatile organic compound and air stripping is a well-established groundwater treatment method for VOC removal
the source water is otherwise relatively clean and does not require an unnecessarily complex solids-treatment train
municipal operation with proven technology preference aligns well with conventional packed-tower aeration systems
this option is commonly selected for gasoline-related groundwater contamination where reliable continuous treatment is needed
potential_risks: MTBE can be more difficult to strip than some other gasoline VOCs, so adequate air-to-water ratio and tower design are important
offgas controls may be needed depending on emission limits
scaling or fouling in aeration equipment can increase maintenance if water chemistry changes

core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb dissolved MTBE onto activated carbon media
core_unit_functions.lead_lag_gac_operation: provide operational redundancy and breakthrough control with staged carbon vessels
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final microbiological protection for distributed drinking water

applicability_rationale: GAC is a proven drinking water technology for organic contaminant control and is familiar to many utilities
lead-lag operation supports stable effluent quality and practical media changeout management
the low turbidity and low DOC water reduce competitive fouling pressure compared with more organic-rich waters
this is a practical alternative where air emissions management is undesirable or where adsorption infrastructure is preferred
potential_risks: MTBE is less strongly adsorbed than many other gasoline hydrocarbons, so carbon usage rate may be higher than expected
breakthrough can occur faster if empty bed contact time is undersized
spent carbon replacement or regeneration logistics must be managed

core_unit_functions.air_stripping: remove the majority of volatile MTBE load by phase transfer to air
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: provide additional polishing for residual organics and improve finished water quality assurance
core_unit_functions.offgas_treatment_as_needed: manage stripped vapor-phase contaminants where required
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of the treated water

applicability_rationale: combined treatment provides a conservative and robust barrier approach for odor-sensitive MTBE contamination
air stripping reduces bulk contaminant load while GAC improves polishing and operational assurance
this train is especially attractive when raw water concentrations fluctuate or when very consistent finished water quality is desired
the approach remains based on established conventional technologies rather than novel treatment methods
potential_risks: higher capital and operating cost than a single primary treatment step
both offgas and spent carbon management may be required
added process complexity may be unnecessary for straightforward single-contaminant cases

core_unit_functions.advanced_oxidation_process: generate hydroxyl radicals to transform MTBE into more treatable byproducts
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_or_biological_: remove oxidation byproducts and provide finishing treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide final drinking water microbial protection

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation has been investigated and applied for persistent organic contaminants including MTBE where conventional transfer or adsorption is insufficient
it can be considered when site-specific goals favor contaminant destruction over phase transfer
polishing is typically included because partial oxidation products may remain and require removal
potential_risks: higher energy and chemical demand than standard aeration or GAC systems
formation of intermediate byproducts requires monitoring and polishing
process optimization and operator attention are greater than in simpler proven municipal systems

core_unit_functions.membrane_treatment_reverse_osmosis: provide broad-spectrum dissolved contaminant reduction through high-pressure membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust product water chemistry after aggressive desalting treatment
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbial safety in finished drinking water

applicability_rationale: RO can be technically considered as a broad contaminant control option
it is generally not the preferred conventional choice for a single VOC contaminant in otherwise acceptable groundwater
this option would more typically be justified only if there were additional salinity or multiple dissolved contaminant problems
potential_risks: unnecessarily high energy use for this straightforward MTBE case
added complexity and stabilization needs make it a poor fit for proven simple municipal operation
